attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic concerned about white patches developing on her hands over the past three months of which she is unhappy with their appearance. She works as a cleaner at a hospital and regularly uses various cleaning products. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She reports no other symptoms.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are well-demarcated, depigmented patches on the dorsum of both hands and around her wrists. Wood's light examination shows bright fluorescence of the affected areas. The rest of her skin examination is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features consistent with vitiligo, an autoimmune condition causing destruction of melanocytes. The characteristic well-demarcated, depigmented patches and Wood's light fluorescence support this diagnosis. While her occupational exposure to chemicals may have triggered or exacerbated the condition, management should focus on treating the condition itself.

A comprehensive approach to managing vitiligo includes lifestyle modifications, non-pharmacological strategies, and pharmacological treatments:

First-line management involves sun protection with SPF 30+ sunscreen and the use of topical corticosteroids for non-sensitive areas, or calcineurin inhibitors (e.g., tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) for sensitive areas like the face. Second-line options include narrowband UVB phototherapy, systemic corticosteroids for rapidly progressive cases, and topical vitamin D analogues to support repigmentation. Cosmetic camouflage options, such as makeup, self-tanning products, or tattoos, can help improve appearance.

Sun protection and topical corticosteroids is the most appropriate initial management. Sun protection prevents burning of the depigmented areas and reduces contrast with surrounding skin. Topical corticosteroids can help halt disease progression and may promote repigmentation in some cases.

Change occupation is not necessary as the first step. While certain chemicals can trigger vitiligo in susceptible individuals, once established, removing the trigger may not reverse the condition. Protective measures like gloves may be advised while continuing work.

Reassurance only is insufficient. While vitiligo is not physically harmful, it can cause significant psychological distress and active management can help prevent progression and potentially achieve repigmentation.

Narrowband UVB phototherapy might be considered for widespread disease that has failed topical therapy, but it is not appropriate as initial management for localised patches. It also requires multiple hospital visits over several months.

Oral antifungal medication would not be appropriate as this is not a fungal infection. While some fungal infections can cause hypopigmentation, the Wood's light findings and well-demarcated nature of the lesions are more consistent with vitiligo.

This approach is tailored to the severity and location of vitiligo and the patient’s preferences.
learningPoints: Vitiligo is an autoimmune condition causing depigmented patches that are more noticeable in darker skin types.
Sun protection and topical corticosteroids are first-line treatment for vitiligo to prevent burning and promote repigmentation.
Occupational exposure to certain chemicals can trigger or worsen vitiligo in susceptible individuals.
references: DermNet NZ. Vitiligo. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/vitiligo
Whitton ME, Ashcroft DM, González U. Therapeutic interventions for vitiligo. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2008;59(4):713-717. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18793940/
